name: TypeScript Essentials
description: Level up your TypeScript — generics, utility types, type narrowing, and patterns that make codebases safer and more expressive.

cards.front: What are generics in TypeScript and why use them?
cards.back:
Generics let you write reusable code that works with multiple types while maintaining type safety.

function identity<T>(value: T): T {
  return value;
}

identity<string>('hello'); // type: string
identity<number>(42);      // type: number

Real-world examples:
• Array<T>, Promise<T>, Map<K, V>
• useState<number>(0)
• function fetch<T>(url: string): Promise<T>

Without generics you'd use 'any', losing type safety. Generics = flexibility + safety.

cards.front: What is the difference between 'type' and 'interface'?
Explain TypeScript utility types: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit.
What is type narrowing in TypeScript?

cards.back:
Both accept any value, but unknown is TYPE-SAFE:

let a: any = 'hello';
a.foo(); // No error — any disables type checking

let b: unknown = 'hello';
b.foo(); // ERROR — must narrow first

if (typeof b === 'string') {
  b.toUpperCase(); // OK after narrowing
}

Rules:
• any: opt out of type system (avoid)
• unknown: "I don't know the type yet" (safe)
• never: "this should never happen" (exhaustiveness)

Use unknown for: API responses, JSON parsing, catch blocks
Use any for: migration from JS, quick prototyping only

cards.front: What are mapped types in TypeScript?
What is 'as const' and when should you use it?
How do you type React component props effectively?
What is the 'never' type and how is it used?
What are type assertions and when are they appropriate?
cards.back:
Type assertions tell TypeScript to treat a value as a specific type:

const input = document.getElementById('name') as HTMLInputElement;
input.value; // Now TS knows it's an input

When appropriate:
• DOM element access (getElementById returns Element | null)
• Working with external data you've validated
• After runtime type checks that TS can't follow

When to AVOID:
• To silence errors you don't understand
• Instead of proper type narrowing
• With 'as any' (defeats the purpose)

Prefer type guards: if (input instanceof HTMLInputElement) { }
Use assertions as a last resort, not a first reach.
